verses.text: And when David was a little past the top of the hill, behold, Ziba the servant of Mephibosheth met him, with a couple of asses saddled, and upon them two hundred loaves of bread, and an hundred bunches of raisins, and an hundred of summer fruits, and a bottle of wine. And the king said unto Ziba, What meanest thou by these? And Ziba said, The asses be for the king's household to ride on; and the bread and summer fruit for the young men to eat; and the wine, that such as be faint in the wilderness may drink. And the king said, And where is thy master's son? And Ziba said unto the king, Behold, he abideth at Jerusalem: for he said, To day shall the house of Israel restore me the kingdom of my father. Then said the king to Ziba, Behold, thine are all that pertained unto Mephibosheth. And Ziba said, I humbly beseech thee that I may find grace in thy sight, my lord, O king. Guidance: David descends the eastern slope of the Mount of Olives. Ziba — Saul's old estate manager, given to Mephibosheth in chapter 9 — meets him with two saddled donkeys laden with provisions (200 loaves, 100 raisin-clusters, 100 summer fruit cakes, a wineskin). The gifts are real and substantial. David asks what they are; Ziba explains their use. Then David asks the crucial question: 'Where is thy master's son?' Ziba's answer is the chapter's lie: 'He abideth at Jerusalem: for he said, Today shall the house of Israel restore me the kingdom of my father.' Ziba slanders Mephibosheth — claiming the lame prince thinks the rebellion is his chance to recover Saul's throne. The accusation is implausible (Mephibosheth is lame, has been at David's table for years, has no political base), but David, in flight and stressed, has no time to investigate. He grants Ziba all of Mephibosheth's estate on the spot. Ziba thanks him: 'I humbly beseech thee that I may find grace in thy sight.' Chapter 19 will eventually reveal that Mephibosheth had actually intended to flee with David but Ziba had taken the donkeys before Mephibosheth could mount one (19:26). Ziba has framed his master to steal the estate. The chapter is showing us how crisis distorts the king's judgment. David, who was a careful judge in chapter 12 and a gentle protector in chapter 9, now makes a sweeping property reassignment without due process. Refugees rarely judge well. The chapter is gentle about David's mistake but does not hide it; chapter 19 will show him trying to walk it back partially when the truth comes out.

And when king David came to Bahurim, behold, thence came out a man of the family of the house of Saul, whose name was Shimei, the son of Gera: he came forth, and cursed still as he came. And he cast stones at David, and at all the servants of king David: and all the people and all the mighty men were on his right hand and on his left. And thus said Shimei when he cursed, Come out, come out, thou bloody man, and thou man of Belial: The LORD hath returned upon thee all the blood of the house of Saul, in whose stead thou hast reigned; and the LORD hath delivered the kingdom into the hand of Absalom thy son: and, behold, thou art taken in thy mischief, because thou art a bloody man. Guidance: At Bahurim — the same town where Phaltiel had wept after Michal was taken from him in chapter 3 — Shimei son of Gera comes out. Shimei is a Saulide-clan Benjamite. He has waited years for the moment David's house would fall. He sees the procession of barefoot weeping men coming down the road, and his suppressed political grudge erupts. He throws stones at the king and his entourage. He hurls dust. He shouts curses. The accusation: 'thou bloody man… man of Belial' (man of worthlessness). 'The LORD hath returned upon thee all the blood of the house of Saul, in whose stead thou hast reigned; and the LORD hath delivered the kingdom into the hand of Absalom thy son: and, behold, thou art taken in thy mischief, because thou art a bloody man.' Shimei's accusation is theologically half-correct. He misreads the cause (the rebellion is not punishment for Saul's blood; David never shed Saul's blood, was scrupulous about it). But the conclusion is half-right — the LORD is indeed chastening David, just for the Bathsheba/Uriah sin Shimei does not know about. The chapter is showing us how an enemy's malice can carry inadvertent truth. Shimei accuses David falsely about Saul; he accuses David accurately about being under God's judgment. The chapter is going to record David's astonishing reception of this hostile mixed accusation.

Then said Abishai the son of Zeruiah unto the king, Why should this dead dog curse my lord the king? let me go over, I pray thee, and take off his head. And the king said, What have I to do with you, ye sons of Zeruiah? so let him curse, because the LORD hath said unto him, Curse David. Who shall then say, Wherefore hast thou done so? And David said to Abishai, and to all his servants, Behold, my son, which came forth of my bowels, seeketh my life: how much more now may this Benjamite do it? let him alone, and let him curse; for the LORD hath bidden him. It may be that the LORD will look on mine affliction, and that the LORD will requite me good for his cursing this day. Guidance: Abishai reaches for his sword. 'Why should this dead dog curse my lord the king? let me go over, I pray thee, and take off his head.' The sons of Zeruiah's instinct: kill the cursing peasant; the king does not need to endure public humiliation. David's restraint is one of the great moments in 2 Samuel. 'What have I to do with you, ye sons of Zeruiah?' The same phrase he used after Abner's murder (3:39) — the rebuke for impetuous violence. Then the theological reasoning. (1) 'Let him curse, because the LORD hath said unto him, Curse David.' David reads even the stones and shouts as the LORD's discipline. He is not certifying Shimei's words as true; he is recognizing that under the LORD's permitted chastening, the cursing is part of the experience. (2) 'My son, which came forth of my bowels, seeketh my life: how much more now may this Benjamite do it?' David puts the curse in proportion. His own son is hunting his life; a stranger's curse is a smaller matter. (3) 'It may be that the LORD will look on mine affliction, and that the LORD will requite me good for his cursing this day.' The most beautiful theological move: David hopes that bearing the unfair curse will move the LORD to grant a future blessing. The chapter is showing us a David who, after Nathan's confrontation in chapter 12, is now able to receive his chastening rather than rage at it. Sometimes the most spiritually mature response to verbal abuse is to receive it as the LORD's instrument and to hope the LORD will turn it into eventual mercy. The chapter is one of the great Old Testament pictures of accepting humiliation in faith.

And as David and his men went by the way, Shimei went along on the hill's side over against him, and cursed as he went, and threw stones at him, and cast dust. And the king, and all the people that were with him, came weary, and refreshed themselves there. Guidance: Shimei keeps pace with the procession from the hillside opposite the road — close enough to throw stones and dust, far enough to evade pursuit. The harassment is continuous. The chapter is showing us how relentless the humiliation is — it is not one outburst, but a sustained accompaniment along the entire trail. David and his men finally arrive 'weary' at a camp-spot and 'refreshed themselves' (probably at the Jordan crossing area, perhaps the same spot called 'the plain' in v28 of chapter 15). Sleep, food, water. The day's worst is over. The king, who has lost his city, his army, and his dignity, is bedded down somewhere in the wilderness, surrounded by his loyalists, restored at least to bare physical functioning. There is a category of rest that becomes a gift in the worst day of one's life — the body's wisdom to recover what the soul will need for tomorrow.

And Absalom, and all the people the men of Israel, came to Jerusalem, and Ahithophel with him. And it came to pass, when Hushai the Archite, David's friend, was come unto Absalom, that Hushai said unto Absalom, God save the king, God save the king. And Absalom said to Hushai, Is this thy kindness to thy friend? why wentest thou not with thy friend? Guidance: Cut to Jerusalem. Absalom and his rebel army enter the city; Ahithophel is at his side. Hushai meets them, performing the planned-defection routine David had instructed in chapter 15. Hushai's greeting is exuberant: 'God save the king, God save the king' (yechi ha-melekh — 'let the king live!'). The double-repetition is deliberate; ancient Near-Eastern coronation acclamations were often doubled for emphasis. Absalom is suspicious. 'Is this thy kindness to thy friend? Why wentest thou not with thy friend?' He is testing Hushai. David's senior counselor showing up to defect immediately is too convenient; Absalom probes whether this is genuine or a setup. The chapter is showing us that even successful rebels are paranoid about defectors — the very people who switched to your side are the people who might switch again. Hushai needs a believable answer. He has one prepared.

And Absalom, and all the people the men of Israel, came to Jerusalem, and Ahithophel with him. Hushai's greeting is exuberant: 'God save the king, God save the king' (yechi ha-melekh — 'let the king live!'). The double-repetition is deliberate; ancient Near-Eastern coronation acclamations were often doubled for emphasis. Absalom is suspicious. 'Is this thy kindness to thy friend? Why wentest thou not with thy friend?' He is testing Hushai. David's senior counselor showing up to defect immediately is too convenient; Absalom probes whether this is genuine or a setup. The chapter is showing us that even successful rebels are paranoid about defectors — the very people who switched to your side are the people who might switch again. Hushai needs a believable answer. He has one prepared.

And Absalom, and all the people the men of Israel, came to Jerusalem, and Ahithophel with him. And it came to pass, when Hushai the Archite, David's friend, was come unto Absalom, that Hushai said unto Absalom, God save the king, God save the king. And Absalom said to Hushai, Is this thy kindness to thy friend? why wentest thou not with thy friend? Guidance: Cut to Jerusalem. Absalom and his rebel army enter the city; Ahithophel is at his side. Hushai meets them, performing the planned-defection routine David had instructed in chapter 15. Hushai's greeting is exuberant: 'God save the king, God save the king' (yechi ha-melekh — 'let the king live!'). The double-repetition is deliberate; ancient Near-Eastern coronation acclamations were often doubled for emphasis. Absalom is suspicious. 'Is this thy kindness to thy friend? Why wentest thou not with thy friend?' He is testing Hushai. David's senior counselor showing up to defect immediately is too convenient; Absalom probes whether this is genuine or a setup. The chapter is showing us that even successful rebels are paranoid about defectors — the very people who switched to your side are the people who might switch again. Hushai needs a believable answer. He has one prepared.

And Hushai said unto Absalom, Nay; but whom the LORD, and this people, and all the men of Israel, choose, his will I be, and with him will I abide. And again, whom should I serve? should I not serve in the presence of his son? as I have served in thy father's presence, so will I be in thy presence. Guidance: Hushai's answer is rhetorically brilliant and deliberately ambiguous. (1) 'Whom the LORD, and this people, and all the men of Israel, choose, his will I be.' Hushai pretends to identify Absalom with the LORD's chosen — but the sentence is constructed so that he can later claim he meant David (whom the LORD had chosen) all along. He has not actually said 'I am for Absalom'; he has said 'I am for whomever the LORD and the people have chosen' — leaving the referent technically undetermined. (2) 'Whom should I serve? Should I not serve in the presence of his son?' This is the appeal to dynastic continuity — I served David, his son inherits, and I serve the son. The framing makes Hushai's defection look natural. (3) 'As I have served in thy father's presence, so will I be in thy presence.' Same loyalty, new pharaoh. Absalom accepts. The chapter is showing us that the LORD has answered David's prayer (15:31, 'turn the counsel of Ahithophel into foolishness') by getting Hushai installed in Absalom's inner circle. The counter-rebellion's brain is now in place inside the rebellion's palace. Tomorrow's chapter 17 will show Hushai using this position to override Ahithophel's first crucial military counsel — buying David the time he needs to escape and reorganize.

Then said Absalom to Ahithophel, Give counsel among you what we shall do. And Ahithophel said unto Absalom, Go in unto thy father's concubines, which he hath left to keep the house; and all Israel shall hear that thou art abhorred of thy father: then shall the hands of all that are with thee be strong. So they spread Absalom a tent upon the top of the house; and Absalom went in unto his father's concubines in the sight of all Israel. Guidance: Absalom's first cabinet meeting in Jerusalem. 'Give counsel among you what we shall do.' He turns to Ahithophel. Ahithophel's first counsel is devastating and politically brilliant. 'Go in unto thy father's concubines.' The recommendation: Absalom should publicly sleep with David's ten concubines whom David had left to keep the palace. The political logic is exact. In ancient Near-Eastern royal culture, sleeping with the previous king's harem was the most explicit act of throne-taking (compare Adonijah's later attempt to claim Abishag, 1 Kgs 2; Reuben's sleeping with Bilhah was read the same way in Gen 35:22 and 49:4). The act would communicate to all Israel two things: (1) Absalom has irreversibly broken with his father — there is no possibility of reconciliation now, and the rebels' commitment to him cannot be hedged; (2) Absalom is the new king de facto, by the most explicit possible signal. 'The hands of all that are with thee shall be strong' — the rebellion solidifies because the participants are now too committed to back out. Then the staging: 'They spread Absalom a tent upon the top of the house; and Absalom went in unto his father's concubines in the sight of all Israel.' On the very roof David had walked when he saw Bathsheba bathing (11:2). The same roof. The act is performed publicly, with a tent providing nominal privacy but the act-itself broadcast across Jerusalem. Nathan's prophecy from 12:11-12 is being fulfilled word for word: 'I will take thy wives before thine eyes, and give them unto thy neighbour, and he shall lie with thy wives in the sight of this sun.' The chapter is recording the precise execution of the LORD's announced sentence. Ahithophel, intending only political advice, has become the unwitting instrument of divine prophecy. The hidden roof-sin of chapter 11 is the public roof-shame of chapter 16. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD's word does land, even if it takes years and runs through unwitting agents. There is no expiration date on the LORD's announced consequences.

And the counsel of Ahithophel, which he counselled in those days, was as if a man had enquired at the oracle of God: so was all the counsel of Ahithophel both with David and with Absalom. Guidance: The chapter's closing footnote. 'The counsel of Ahithophel… was as if a man had enquired at the oracle of God.' The praise is the highest possible — Ahithophel's strategic intuition was so accurate that consulting him was indistinguishable from consulting the LORD through the Urim and Thummim. He was the kingdom's most reliable political mind. And he served 'both with David and with Absalom' — his judgment was the same caliber in both administrations. The note is critical for what follows. It tells us why David's prayer in chapter 15 ('turn the counsel of Ahithophel into foolishness') had to be specifically about Ahithophel's mind. Any ordinary counselor on Absalom's side would have given competent strategic advice; Ahithophel's advice was God-grade. The only way to defeat his next counsel (which will be to pursue David that very night with a fast strike force, killing him before he can regroup — chapter 17) is for Hushai to override it from inside. The chapter is setting up the strategic miracle that will end the rebellion. David's prayed-for confusion of Ahithophel is going to land tomorrow, in chapter 17, when Absalom inexplicably prefers Hushai's worse counsel over Ahithophel's better one. The chapter ends with the rebellion at its peak: Absalom on the throne, Ahithophel at his side, the country aligned with the new king, the ten concubines violated, David fleeing in the wilderness. Tomorrow, chapter 17, the LORD's promised reversal will begin. The chapter is the rebellion's noon; tomorrow will be its sunset.
